deck_title: Trait Variations: Why Animals Look and Act the Way They Do
subject: Science
differentiation: Two reading levels; sentence frames for emerging readers; an extension question for early finishers.

slides.title: Trait Variations
bullets: Why animals look and act the way they do
slides.notes: Suggested time: 1 min. Welcome routine while the class settles.

slides.title: Do Now
bullets: Look at the mother duck and her ducklings on the board.
What do you notice that looks the same? What looks different?
slides.notes: Suggested time: 4 min. Opening check-for-understanding routine. Emerging readers: offer the sentence frame 'The ducklings and their mother both have ___.'
slides.check_for_understanding: Can students name at least one shared trait between parent and offspring?

slides.title: What Shapes a Trait?
bullets: A trait is something you can observe about a living thing.
Traits can be inherited from parents or shaped by environment.
slides.notes: Suggested time: 6 min. Anchor vocabulary: 'inherited' vs. 'environmental' trait. Example: an arctic fox's white coat matches its snowy home. On-level readers define each term in their own words; emerging readers sort trait cards into two piles.

slides.title: Activity: Sort the Trait
bullets: 2. List two traits you can observe.
4. Share your reasoning with your partner.
slides.notes: Suggested time: 12 min. The step-by-step routine pulled from the lesson plan. Extension for early finishers: find a trait that could be shaped by both inheritance and environment.

slides.title: Wrap Up
bullets: Environmental traits are shaped by where a living thing lives.
slides.notes: Suggested time: 3 min. Exit ticket: each student writes one inherited trait and one environmental trait, using an animal of their choice.
